deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered across any of the three conversations.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1 Turn 9-10, Transcript 2 Turn 8-10, Transcript 3 Turn 9-10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students improve their justifications (e.g., T1 Turn 9, T2 Turn 8, T3 Turn 9), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic method and guided discovery, consistently refusing to give answers and instead prompting students to reason through evidence, comparisons, and justifications. Scaffolding is well-executed, building on student progress turn by turn. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why questions rather than answers) and the consistent failure to prompt reflection after student improvement. Communication is concise and appropriately supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led through targeted questions. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on each student's prior responses and diagnoses confusion before redirecting. 1.3 loses 4 points: no tutoring rationale is ever offered across all three conversations (1.3.A.a), and after students improve their justifications the tutor consistently moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality is strong with no redundancy or off-topic drift. Communication is bite-sized and supportive throughout.
